Kernels to Regularization Tutorial

From Kernels to Regularization

A beginner-friendly tutorial, built from the ground up

Based on the lecture decks "Machine Learning — Kernel Theory" and "Machine Learning — Regularization" (A. Lehrmann, THWS). This tutorial keeps the exact logical flow of the slides but fills in every gap — especially the math — and adds intuition and pictures so you can actually learn it, not just recognize it.

How to read this

The two lectures tell one continuous story. It goes like this:

The whole journey on one line. We start from an abstract object (a kernel), discover it secretly lives in a special space of functions (an RKHS), use that structure to prove a theorem that makes learning tractable (the representer theorem), and finally turn it into a usable algorithm (kernel ridge regression). Regularization is the glue that makes all of it generalize.

Each section builds on the previous one, so read in order. Boxes like the one below are intuition first, formalism second — read the box, then the math will feel inevitable.

In plain words. A kernel is a function that measures how similar two data points are. The astonishing fact this whole tutorial builds toward: choosing a similarity function is secretly the same thing as choosing an infinite-dimensional space of features — and you never have to touch that space directly.

Prerequisites: vectors, matrices, dot products, gradients, and the idea of fitting a line by minimizing squared error. Everything else is built here.

Notation cheat-sheet (skim now, refer back later):

Symbol Meaning
𝒳\mathcal{X} input space (where data points xx live, often ℝd\mathbb{R}^d)
ℱ\mathcal{F} feature space (a Hilbert space of functions)
ϕ:𝒳→ℱ\phi:\mathcal{X}\to\mathcal{F} feature map (lifts a point into feature space)
k(x,x′)k(x,x') kernel: a number measuring similarity of xx and x′x'
⟨⋅,⋅⟩\langle\cdot,\cdot\rangle inner product (generalized dot product)
|v|=⟨v,v⟩|v| = \sqrt{\langle v,v\rangle} norm (length)
ww weight vector of a linear model
α\alpha dual coefficients (weights on data points)
K=K(X,X)K = K(X,X) kernel matrix, Kij=k(xi,xj)K_{ij}=k(x_i,x_j)
n,dn,\ d number of training points, number of features
λ\lambda regularization strength

Part 0 — Recap: where we left off

Last time we did linear classification: take the output of a linear regression model w⊤xw^{\top}x and map it to a label.

ŷi=sign(w⊤xi)∈{−1,+1}(𝐝𝐞𝐭𝐞𝐫𝐦𝐢𝐧𝐢𝐬𝐭𝐢𝐜),ŷi=p(yi=+1∣xi)=σ(w⊤xi)∈[0,1](𝐩𝐫𝐨𝐛𝐚𝐛𝐢𝐥𝐢𝐬𝐭𝐢𝐜), \hat{y}_i = \operatorname{sign}(w^{\top}x_i)\in\{-1,+1\}\quad(\textbf{deterministic}), \qquad \hat{y}_i = p(y_i=+1\mid x_i)=\sigma(w^{\top}x_i)\in[0,1]\quad(\textbf{probabilistic}),

where σ(z)=11+e−z\sigma(z)=\dfrac{1}{1+e^{-z}} is the logistic (sigmoid) function.

Which loss do we minimize? Squared error is great for regression but wrong for classification: it punishes predictions that are confidently correct. Instead we use one of these two, written in terms of the margin m=yiw⊤xim = y_i\,w^{\top}x_i (positive = correct and confident):

ℒhinge(w)=∑i=1nmax{0,1−yiw⊤xi},ℒlog(w)=∑i=1nlog(1+exp(−yiw⊤xi)). \mathcal{L}_{\text{hinge}}(w)=\sum_{i=1}^{n}\max\{0,\,1-y_i w^{\top}x_i\}, \qquad \mathcal{L}_{\text{log}}(w)=\sum_{i=1}^{n}\log\!\big(1+\exp(-y_i w^{\top}x_i)\big).

The ideal classification loss is the 0–1 loss (grey): pay 1 for a mistake, 0 otherwise. But it is flat and discontinuous, so gradient methods can't optimize it. Hinge (blue) and logistic (orange) are smooth, convex surrogates that go to zero as the margin grows. Squared loss (red dashed) is a bad choice — it blows up even when a point is correctly classified with a large margin.

The punchline that sets up everything to come: kernels (e.g. RBF, polynomial) let us do linear classification efficiently in very high-dimensional feature spaces. The trick is to optimize a vector α\alpha of weights-on-data-points instead of a weight vector ww, using the relationship

w=Z⊤α,Z=(ϕ(x1),…,ϕ(xn))⊤. w = Z^{\top}\alpha,\qquad Z = \big(\phi(x_1),\dots,\phi(x_n)\big)^{\top}.

Why is that a good idea? Because ww may live in a space with millions (or infinitely many) dimensions, but α\alpha only has nn entries — one per training point. This tutorial explains why we are allowed to do that, and what the mysterious ϕ\phi and kk really are.

Why bother lifting to a high-dimensional space at all? Because data that a straight line can't separate often becomes separable after a nonlinear lift.

Left: two classes arranged as an inner blob and an outer ring. No straight line separates them. Right: apply the feature map ϕ(x)=(x12,2x1x2,x22)\phi(x)=(x_1^2,\ \sqrt{2}\,x_1x_2,\ x_2^2). In this lifted space the two classes fall on different "heights," and a flat plane separates them perfectly. A linear method in feature space = a nonlinear method in the original space.

Part 1 — The mathematical playground: linear algebra recap

Before we can talk about kernels we need the right stage. That stage is built from four nested ideas: vector space → inner-product space → normed space → metric space. Each one adds a new ability.

1.1 Vector spaces

Intuition. A vector space is any collection of objects you can add together and scale by numbers, where these operations behave the way you'd expect. "Objects" doesn't have to mean arrows — it can mean functions, as we'll see next.

A real vector space (ℝ\mathbb{R}-vector space) is a set VV with two operations,

$$

  • : V\times V\to V \qquad\text{and}\qquad \cdot : \mathbb{R}\times V \to V, $$

such that:

  • Addition is associative and commutative, has an identity element 00 (with v+0=vv+0=v), and every vv has an inverse −v-v (with v+(−v)=0v+(-v)=0).
  • Scalar multiplication satisfies, for all u,v∈Vu,v\in V and a,b∈ℝa,b\in\mathbb{R}:
    • identity: 1⋅v=v1\cdot v = v,
    • compatibility with multiplication: (ab)v=a(bv)(ab)v = a(bv),
    • distributivity: a(u+v)=au+ava(u+v)=au+av and (a+b)v=av+bv(a+b)v = av + bv.

The familiar example — ℝd\mathbb{R}^d. The set V={(v1,…,vd)∣vi∈ℝ}V=\{(v_1,\dots,v_d)\mid v_i\in\mathbb{R}\} with componentwise operations

(u1,…,ud)+(v1,…,vd)=(u1+v1,…,ud+vd),a⋅(v1,…,vd)=(av1,…,avd) (u_1,\dots,u_d)+(v_1,\dots,v_d)=(u_1+v_1,\dots,u_d+v_d), \qquad a\cdot(v_1,\dots,v_d)=(av_1,\dots,av_d)

is the vector space ℝd\mathbb{R}^d. This is the "arrows" you already know.

1.2 Functions are vectors, too

Here is the conceptual leap the whole subject rests on.

Key idea. A function is a vector. If you can add two functions and scale a function — and you can — then a set of functions is a vector space. Instead of a list of dd numbers, a function is like a list of infinitely many numbers: its value at every input point.

Let 𝒳\mathcal{X} be any set. The set of all real-valued functions on it,

V={f:𝒳→ℝ}, V=\{f:\mathcal{X}\to\mathbb{R}\},

with the operations defined pointwise,

[f+g](x)=f(x)+g(x),[a⋅f](x)=a⋅f(x), [f+g](x)=f(x)+g(x), \qquad [a\cdot f](x)=a\cdot f(x),

is an ℝ\mathbb{R}-vector space. (Check the axioms if you like — they follow immediately from the same axioms for real numbers.)

A more refined example we'll need is the space of square-integrable functions. Let 𝒳=[a,b]\mathcal{X}=[a,b] be a real interval and define

V={f:[a,b]→ℝ|∫abf(x)2dx<∞}. V=\Big\{f:[a,b]\to\mathbb{R}\ \Big|\ \int_a^b f(x)^2\,dx < \infty\Big\}.

With the same pointwise operations, this forms the vector space L2([a,b])L^2([a,b]). The condition ∫f2<∞\int f^2<\infty just says the function doesn't have "too much energy" — it keeps the integrals we're about to define finite.

1.3 Inner-product spaces

Adding and scaling isn't enough; we also want geometry — angles, lengths, "how aligned are these two vectors?" That is what an inner product provides.

Intuition. An inner product is a generalized dot product: feed it two vectors, get back one number measuring how much they point the same way. Big positive = aligned; zero = orthogonal (perpendicular).

An inner-product space is a vector space VV with an operation ⟨⋅,⋅⟩:V×V→ℝ\langle\cdot,\cdot\rangle:V\times V\to\mathbb{R} satisfying:

  • symmetry: ⟨u,v⟩=⟨v,u⟩\langle u,v\rangle=\langle v,u\rangle,
  • linearity: ⟨au+bv,w⟩=a⟨u,w⟩+b⟨v,w⟩\langle au+bv,\,w\rangle = a\langle u,w\rangle + b\langle v,w\rangle,
  • positive-definiteness: ⟨v,v⟩>0\langle v,v\rangle > 0 for every v≠0v\neq 0.

From an inner product you get, for free, two more layers of structure:

∥v∥=⟨v,v⟩⏟𝐧𝐨𝐫𝐦 (𝐥𝐞𝐧𝐠𝐭𝐡)⇒d(u,v)=∥u−v∥⏟𝐦𝐞𝐭𝐫𝐢𝐜 (𝐝𝐢𝐬𝐭𝐚𝐧𝐜𝐞). \underbrace{\|v\| = \sqrt{\langle v,v\rangle}}_{\textbf{norm (length)}} \qquad\Longrightarrow\qquad \underbrace{d(u,v) = \|u-v\|}_{\textbf{metric (distance)}}.

So (V,∥⋅∥)(V,\|\cdot\|) is a normed space and (V,d(⋅,⋅))(V,d(\cdot,\cdot)) is a metric space. One object — the inner product — hands you length, distance, and angle all at once. (The angle θ\theta between u,vu,v comes from cosθ=⟨u,v⟩/(∥u∥∥v∥)\cos\theta = \langle u,v\rangle/(\|u\|\,\|v\|).)

1.4 Three inner products you must recognize

The same abstract definition covers finite vectors and functions. That unity is the reason kernels work.

Example 1 — the space ℝd\mathbb{R}^d with the ordinary dot product:

⟨u,v⟩=u⊤v=∑i=1duivi. \langle u,v\rangle = u^{\top}v = \sum_{i=1}^d u_i v_i.

This induces the familiar Euclidean (L2L_2) norm

∥v∥2=(∑i=1dvi2)1/2. \|v\|_2 = \Big(\sum_{i=1}^d v_i^2\Big)^{1/2}.

Example 2 — the function space L2([a,b])L^2([a,b]) with the "continuous dot product," where the sum over coordinates becomes an integral:

⟨f,g⟩=∫abf(x)g(x)dx,∥f∥2=(∫abf(x)2dx)1/2. \langle f,g\rangle = \int_a^b f(x)\,g(x)\,dx, \qquad \|f\|_2 = \Big(\int_a^b f(x)^2\,dx\Big)^{1/2}.

See the analogy. A vector in ℝd\mathbb{R}^d has components v1,…,vdv_1,\dots,v_d; the dot product multiplies matching components and sums. A function has a "component" f(x)f(x) at every point xx; the inner product multiplies matching values and integrates. Same idea, one has finitely many components, the other infinitely many.

Example 3 — the Sobolev space H1([a,b])H^1([a,b]), functions whose derivative is also square-integrable, H1={f∈L2([a,b]):f′∈L2([a,b])}H^1=\{f\in L^2([a,b]): f'\in L^2([a,b])\}, with the Sobolev inner product

⟨f,g⟩=∫abf(x)g(x)dx+∫abf′(x)g′(x)dx. \langle f,g\rangle = \int_a^b f(x)g(x)\,dx + \int_a^b f'(x)g'(x)\,dx.

This one also measures agreement of slopes, not just values — a hint that inner products can encode "smoothness," which is exactly what a good regularizer wants.

Part 2 — Reproducing Kernel Hilbert Spaces (RKHS)

2.1 The mystery we're solving

We keep seeing four objects floating around, and their relationship is still fuzzy:

  • the input space 𝒳\mathcal{X},
  • a feature space ℱ\mathcal{F},
  • a feature map ϕ:𝒳→ℱ\phi:\mathcal{X}\to\mathcal{F},
  • a kernel k:𝒳×𝒳→ℝk:\mathcal{X}\times\mathcal{X}\to\mathbb{R}, and the inner product ⟨ϕ(xi),ϕ(xj)⟩ℱ\langle\phi(x_i),\phi(x_j)\rangle_{\mathcal{F}}.

We want the clean identity

k(x,x′)=⟨ϕ(x),ϕ(x′)⟩ℱ∀x,x′∈𝒳 \boxed{\,k(x,x') = \langle \phi(x),\phi(x')\rangle_{\mathcal{F}}\quad\forall x,x'\in\mathcal{X}\,}

— "the kernel is the inner product of features." The goal of this Part is to show where ϕ\phi and kk come from and why this identity holds automatically. Let's explore.

2.2 Hilbert spaces = inner-product spaces with no holes

We build ℱ\mathcal{F} as a Hilbert space of functions. A Hilbert space is an inner-product space that is also complete.

Intuition for completeness. "No missing points." If a sequence of vectors is bunching up (its elements get arbitrarily close to each other), then the limit it's converging to is actually in the space. Rational numbers are not complete (1,1.4,1.41,1.414,…1,1.4,1.41,1.414,\dots bunches up toward 2\sqrt2, which is missing). Real numbers are. Completeness is what lets us safely take limits — and hence guarantee that minimizers of our loss functions exist.

So (ℱ,⟨⋅,⋅⟩ℱ)(\mathcal{F},\langle\cdot,\cdot\rangle_{\mathcal{F}}) being a Hilbert space over ℝ\mathbb{R} means: it's a vector space (we can add and scale), it has an inner product (we can measure angles, lengths, distances), and it is complete (no missing limits). Formally, completeness says: for every sequence {fi}i=1∞\{f_i\}_{i=1}^{\infty} in ℱ\mathcal{F} that is Cauchy — for every r>0r>0 there is an N∈ℕN\in\mathbb{N} such that d(fn,fm)ℱ<rd(f_n,f_m)_{\mathcal{F}} < r for all m,n>Nm,n>N — the sequence has a limit that also lies in ℱ\mathcal{F}.

Now the crucial extra condition. Let (ℱ,⟨⋅,⋅⟩ℱ)(\mathcal{F},\langle\cdot,\cdot\rangle_{\mathcal{F}}) be a Hilbert space whose elements are functions f:𝒳→ℝf:\mathcal{X}\to\mathbb{R} (so s1f1+s2f2s_1 f_1 + s_2 f_2, ⟨f1,f2⟩ℱ\langle f_1,f_2\rangle_{\mathcal{F}} and ∥f1∥ℱ\|f_1\|_{\mathcal{F}} all make sense). Define, for a fixed input point xx, the evaluation functional

evalx:ℱ→ℝ,evalx(f)=f(x), \operatorname{eval}_x:\mathcal{F}\to\mathbb{R},\qquad \operatorname{eval}_x(f) = f(x),

which just reports the value of a function at xx. We call ℱ\mathcal{F} a Reproducing Kernel Hilbert Space (RKHS) if evalx\operatorname{eval}_x is continuous for every x∈𝒳x\in\mathcal{X}.

Continuity at ff means: for all ε>0\varepsilon>0 there exists δ>0\delta>0 such that

dℱ(f′,f)<δ⇒|evalx(f′)−evalx(f)|=|f′(x)−f(x)|<ε. d_{\mathcal{F}}(f',f)<\delta \ \Longrightarrow\ |\operatorname{eval}_x(f')-\operatorname{eval}_x(f)| = |f'(x)-f(x)| < \varepsilon.

What this condition buys us. It says: if two functions are close in the space's norm, then their values at every point are close. In other words, "being nearby as functions" implies "agreeing pointwise." That well-behavedness is precisely what makes pointwise evaluation — the thing we do when we make a prediction f(x)f(x) — trustworthy.

2.3 The reproducing property (this is where the kernel is born)

We now pull the kernel out of thin air using one classical theorem.

Riesz Representation Theorem. In a Hilbert space ℱ\mathcal{F}, every continuous linear functional ψ∈ℱ*\psi\in\mathcal{F}^{*} can be written as an inner product against a single fixed vector: there exists a unique fψ∈ℱf_\psi\in\mathcal{F} with

ψ[f]=⟨f,fψ⟩ℱ∀f∈ℱ. \psi[f] = \langle f, f_\psi\rangle_{\mathcal{F}}\qquad \forall f\in\mathcal{F}.

Here ℱ*\mathcal{F}^{*} is the dual space of ℱ\mathcal{F} — the space of all linear and continuous maps from ℱ\mathcal{F} to ℝ\mathbb{R}.

Intuition. Every "linear measurement" you can perform on a Hilbert space is secretly a dot product with some fixed vector. The measurement device is a vector.

Apply this to evalx\operatorname{eval}_x. It is linear (evaluating af+bga f + b g at xx gives af(x)+bg(x)a f(x)+b g(x)), and in an RKHS it is continuous — so Riesz applies. There must exist a special element, call it kx∈ℱk_x\in\mathcal{F}, such that

f(x)=evalx(f)=⟨f,kx⟩ℱ∀f∈ℱ. \boxed{\,f(x) = \operatorname{eval}_x(f) = \langle f,\,k_x\rangle_{\mathcal{F}}\qquad\forall f\in\mathcal{F}.\,}

This is the reproducing property: taking the inner product of any function ff with the magic element kxk_x "reproduces" the value f(x)f(x).

Now here's the move. The representer kxk_x is itself a function 𝒳→ℝ\mathcal{X}\to\mathbb{R} (it lives in ℱ\mathcal{F}, and ℱ\mathcal{F} is a space of functions). So we can evaluate it at another point x′x'. Using the reproducing property again, with f=kxf=k_x:

kx(x′)=evalx′(kx)=⟨kx,kx′⟩ℱ. k_x(x') = \operatorname{eval}_{x'}(k_x) = \langle k_x,\,k_{x'}\rangle_{\mathcal{F}}.

Define the kernel and the feature map by

k(x,x′):=kx(x′)=⟨kx,kx′⟩ℱ,ϕ(x):=kx. k(x,x') := k_x(x') = \langle k_x,k_{x'}\rangle_{\mathcal{F}}, \qquad \phi(x) := k_x .

Substituting one into the other gives exactly the identity we wanted:

k(x,x′)=⟨ϕ(x),ϕ(x′)⟩ℱ. \boxed{\,k(x,x') = \langle \phi(x),\phi(x')\rangle_{\mathcal{F}}.\,}

Mystery solved. The feature map is "map a point xx to its evaluation-representer function kxk_x," and the kernel is "the inner product of two such representers." You never chose ϕ\phi and kk separately — the Hilbert space handed them to you together.

2.4 The reproducing kernel is symmetric and positive-definite

Two properties fall out for free, and they matter enormously.

Symmetry is immediate from symmetry of the inner product:

k(x,x′)=⟨kx,kx′⟩ℱ=⟨kx′,kx⟩ℱ=k(x′,x). k(x,x') = \langle k_x,k_{x'}\rangle_{\mathcal{F}} = \langle k_{x'},k_x\rangle_{\mathcal{F}} = k(x',x).

Positive-definiteness. For any finite set of points {xi}i=1n\{x_i\}_{i=1}^n and any coefficients {ci}i=1n⊂ℝ\{c_i\}_{i=1}^n\subset\mathbb{R},

∑i,jcicjk(xi,xj)=∑i,jcicj⟨kxi,kxj⟩ℱ=⟨∑icikxi,∑jcjkxj⟩ℱ=∥∑icikxi∥ℱ2≥0. \sum_{i,j} c_i c_j\, k(x_i,x_j) = \sum_{i,j} c_i c_j\, \langle k_{x_i},k_{x_j}\rangle_{\mathcal{F}} = \Big\langle \sum_i c_i k_{x_i},\ \sum_j c_j k_{x_j}\Big\rangle_{\mathcal{F}} = \Big\|\sum_i c_i k_{x_i}\Big\|_{\mathcal{F}}^2 \ \ge\ 0.

The middle step is just linearity of the inner product; the last is the definition of the norm. A squared length can't be negative — so the kernel is positive-definite.

Why we care. These two properties are the entire "admission ticket." Any function kk that is symmetric and positive-definite is a legal kernel, and (next section) automatically comes with its own hidden feature space. Positive-definiteness is also what guarantees the kernel matrix KK is invertible-friendly, which we'll use when we solve for α\alpha.

Key take-away (direction 1).

For every RKHS (ℱ,⟨⋅,⋅⟩ℱ)(\mathcal{F},\langle\cdot,\cdot\rangle_{\mathcal{F}}) we can construct a unique symmetric, positive-definite kernel kℱk_{\mathcal{F}}. It is called the reproducing kernel of that space.

A concrete example. Take the dual space of ℝd\mathbb{R}^d: (ℝd)*={fα:ℝd→ℝ,x↦α⊤x}(\mathbb{R}^d)^{*}=\{f_\alpha:\mathbb{R}^d\to\mathbb{R},\ x\mapsto \alpha^{\top}x\} — all linear maps — with inner product ⟨fα,fβ⟩(ℝd)*=⟨α,β⟩ℝd\langle f_\alpha,f_\beta\rangle_{(\mathbb{R}^d)^{*}} = \langle\alpha,\beta\rangle_{\mathbb{R}^d}. For a point xx, the Riesz representer of evalx\operatorname{eval}_x is fxf_x, because

fα(x)=⟨fα,fx⟩(ℝd)*=α⊤xfor all fα. f_\alpha(x) = \langle f_\alpha, f_x\rangle_{(\mathbb{R}^d)^{*}} = \alpha^{\top}x \qquad\text{for all } f_\alpha.

Then, for another point x′x',

fx(x′)=⟨fx,fx′⟩(ℝd)*=x⊤x′. f_x(x') = \langle f_x, f_{x'}\rangle_{(\mathbb{R}^d)^{*}} = x^{\top}x'.

So the reproducing kernel of (ℝd)*(\mathbb{R}^d)^{*} is the linear kernel k(x,x′)=x⊤x′k(x,x')=x^{\top}x'. The plainest kernel you know is just an RKHS in disguise.

2.5 Moore–Aronszajn: the two-way street

Direction 1 said RKHS ⇒\Rightarrow kernel. In practice we usually want to go the other way: pick a convenient similarity function and get a powerful feature space for free. Constructing an RKHS by hand to get a "useful" kernel is hard. Fortunately we don't have to.

Moore–Aronszajn Theorem. If k:𝒳×𝒳→ℝk:\mathcal{X}\times\mathcal{X}\to\mathbb{R} is a symmetric, positive-definite kernel, then there exists a unique RKHS (ℱ,⟨⋅,⋅⟩ℱ)(\mathcal{F},\langle\cdot,\cdot\rangle_{\mathcal{F}}) whose reproducing kernel is kk.

Putting both directions together gives a perfect correspondence:

symmetric positive-definite kernel k⇔a unique RKHS ℱ \boxed{\ \text{symmetric positive-definite kernel } k \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad \text{a unique RKHS } \mathcal{F}\ }

This is the whole magic of kernel methods. To work in some rich, possibly infinite-dimensional feature space ℱ\mathcal{F}, you do not build ℱ\mathcal{F}, you do not compute ϕ(x)\phi(x). You just write down a symmetric positive-definite function k(x,x′)k(x,x'). The space exists, the features exist, and every inner product you'll ever need is just a call to kk. This is the kernel trick.

2.6 Kernels you'll actually use

The slides prove the theory with the linear kernel; in practice you pick from a small menu. All are symmetric and positive-definite, so each secretly names an RKHS.

Kernel Formula Feature space
Linear k(x,x′)=x⊤x′k(x,x')=x^{\top}x' ℝd\mathbb{R}^d itself
Polynomial (degree pp) k(x,x′)=(x⊤x′+c)pk(x,x')=(x^{\top}x'+c)^{p} all monomials up to degree pp
RBF / Gaussian k(x,x′)=exp(−|x−x′|2/2σ2)k(x,x')=\exp\!\big(-|x-x'|^2/2\sigma^2\big) infinite-dimensional

Left: the RBF (Gaussian) kernel as a similarity score — it's 11 when x=x′x=x' and decays smoothly with distance; the bandwidth σ\sigma sets how quickly. Right: place one kernel "bump" k(⋅,xi)k(\cdot,x_i) on each data point and take a weighted sum f(x)=∑iαik(x,xi)f(x)=\sum_i\alpha_i k(x,x_i). Any function in the RBF-RKHS looks like this. Hold onto this picture — the representer theorem is about to tell us the best predictor always has exactly this form.

Part 3 — The Representer Theorem

We now have the arena (RKHS) and its currency (kernels). Time to learn. But a function space can be infinite-dimensional — how could we possibly search it for the best predictor? The representer theorem is the answer, and it is the theoretical heart of the whole course.

3.1 Risk, empirical risk, and the objective

Consider a family of hypotheses ℋ={f:𝒳→ℝ}\mathcal{H}=\{f:\mathcal{X}\to\mathbb{R}\} (for linear regression, ℋ={fw:x↦w⊤x}\mathcal{H}=\{f_w: x\mapsto w^{\top}x\}). The risk of a hypothesis is its expected loss over the true data distribution p(x,y)p(x,y):

ℛ[f]=𝔼(x,y)∼p(x,y)[ℒ(f(x),y)],ℒ:ℝ×ℝ→ℝ≥0. \mathcal{R}[f] = \mathbb{E}_{(x,y)\sim p(x,y)}\big[\mathcal{L}(f(x),y)\big], \qquad \mathcal{L}:\mathbb{R}\times\mathbb{R}\to\mathbb{R}_{\ge 0}.

The dream is f*=argminf∈ℋℛ[f]f^{*}=\arg\min_{f\in\mathcal{H}}\mathcal{R}[f]. The problem: p(x,y)p(x,y) is unknown — we only have samples. So we assume our data is drawn i.i.d., {(xi,yi)}i=1n∼i.i.d.p(x,y)\{(x_i,y_i)\}_{i=1}^{n}\overset{\text{i.i.d.}}{\sim}p(x,y), and minimize the regularized empirical risk instead:

f*=argminf∈ℱ1n∑i=1nℒ(f(xi),yi)⏟fit the data+g(∥f∥ℱ)⏟stay simple \boxed{\,f^{*} = \arg\min_{f\in\mathcal{F}}\ \underbrace{\frac1n\sum_{i=1}^{n}\mathcal{L}(f(x_i),y_i)}_{\text{fit the data}}\ +\ \underbrace{g(\|f\|_{\mathcal{F}})}_{\text{stay simple}}\,}

where g:[0,∞)→ℝg:[0,\infty)\to\mathbb{R} is a strictly increasing function of the norm. The first term rewards fitting the training data; the second penalizes "large" (complicated) functions. That second term is regularization — the subject of Part 4 — and it is exactly the ingredient that makes the coming theorem work.

3.2 The theorem

Representer Theorem. Given a kernel k:𝒳×𝒳→ℝk:\mathcal{X}\times\mathcal{X}\to\mathbb{R} with RKHS (ℱ,⟨⋅,⋅⟩ℱ)(\mathcal{F},\langle\cdot,\cdot\rangle_{\mathcal{F}}), the minimizer f*f^{*} of the regularized empirical risk in ℱ\mathcal{F} has the form f*(⋅)=∑i=1nαik(⋅,xi),αi∈ℝ. f^{*}(\cdot) = \sum_{i=1}^{n}\alpha_i\,k(\cdot,x_i),\qquad \alpha_i\in\mathbb{R}.

Read that again: the best function in a possibly infinite-dimensional space is a finite sum — one term per training point. The search collapses from "all of ℱ\mathcal{F}" to "just the nn numbers α1,…,αn\alpha_1,\dots,\alpha_n." That is why kernel methods are computable at all.

Before the proof, one identity we'll reuse. With the feature map ϕ(x)=k(⋅,x)\phi(x)=k(\cdot,x), the reproducing property gives

[ϕ(x)](x′)=k(x′,x)=⟨ϕ(x′),ϕ(x)⟩ℱ, [\phi(x)](x') = k(x',x) = \langle \phi(x'),\phi(x)\rangle_{\mathcal{F}},

and therefore, writing f*f^{*} in the boxed form,

f*(x)=∑i=1nαik(x,xi)=∑i=1nαik(xi,x)=∑i=1nαi⟨ϕ(xi),ϕ(x)⟩ℱ=⟨∑i=1nαiϕ(xi)⏟=:v*,ϕ(x)⟩ℱ. f^{*}(x)=\sum_{i=1}^n\alpha_i k(x,x_i)=\sum_{i=1}^n\alpha_i k(x_i,x)=\sum_{i=1}^n\alpha_i\langle\phi(x_i),\phi(x)\rangle_{\mathcal{F}}=\Big\langle \underbrace{\sum_{i=1}^n\alpha_i\phi(x_i)}_{=:v^{*}},\ \phi(x)\Big\rangle_{\mathcal{F}}.

So f*f^{*} corresponds to a single feature-space vector v*=∑iαiϕ(xi)v^{*}=\sum_i\alpha_i\phi(x_i) living in the span of the training features.

3.3 Proof (in three clean steps)

The proof is a beautiful "orthogonality" argument. We show the optimal ff can have no component outside the span of the training data.

Step 1 — Orthogonal decomposition. We minimize over the RKHS ℋ=ℱ\mathcal{H}=\mathcal{F}. Consider the feature map ϕ:𝒳→ℱ,x↦k(⋅,x)\phi:\mathcal{X}\to\mathcal{F},\ x\mapsto k(\cdot,x), which satisfies [ϕ(x)](x′)=k(x′,x)=⟨ϕ(x′),ϕ(x)⟩ℱ[\phi(x)](x')=k(x',x)=\langle\phi(x'),\phi(x)\rangle_{\mathcal{F}}. Let

S:=span({ϕ(xi)}i=1n) S := \operatorname{span}\big(\{\phi(x_i)\}_{i=1}^{n}\big)

be the (at most nn-dimensional) subspace spanned by the training features. Any f∈ℱf\in\mathcal{F} splits uniquely into a part inside SS and a part orthogonal to it:

f=u+v,u∈S,v∈S⊥,⟨u,v⟩=0. f = u + v,\qquad u\in S,\quad v\in S^{\perp},\quad \langle u,v\rangle = 0.

The blue plane is SS, spanned by the training features. Any candidate function ff (blue arrow) is the sum of its shadow uu inside the plane (green) and a leftover vv sticking straight out of it (orange). The proof shows the leftover vv never helps — so the best ff lies flat in the plane.

Step 2 — the fit term ignores vv. Evaluating ff at a training point xjx_j uses the reproducing property (a Riesz representation), then orthogonality:

f(xj)=⟨f,ϕ(xj)⟩=⟨u+v,ϕ(xj)⟩=⟨u,ϕ(xj)⟩+⟨v,ϕ(xj)⟩⏟=0since ϕ(xj)∈S,v⊥S. f(x_j)=\langle f,\phi(x_j)\rangle = \langle u+v,\,\phi(x_j)\rangle = \langle u,\phi(x_j)\rangle + \underbrace{\langle v,\phi(x_j)\rangle}_{=\,0\ \text{since }\phi(x_j)\in S,\ v\perp S}.

Since u∈Su\in S we can write u=∑iαiϕ(xi)u=\sum_{i}\alpha_i\phi(x_i), so

f(xj)=∑i=1nαi⟨ϕ(xi),ϕ(xj)⟩=∑i=1nαik(xi,xj). f(x_j)=\sum_{i=1}^n\alpha_i\,\langle \phi(x_i),\phi(x_j)\rangle = \sum_{i=1}^n\alpha_i\,k(x_i,x_j).

The predictions at all training points depend only on uu — not on vv. Hence the entire data-fit term 1n∑jℒ(f(xj),yj)\frac1n\sum_j\mathcal{L}(f(x_j),y_j) is unchanged if we drop vv. We may as well assume

f(⋅)=u(⋅)=∑i=1nαi[ϕ(xi)](⋅)=∑i=1nαik(⋅,xi). f(\cdot)=u(\cdot)=\sum_{i=1}^n\alpha_i[\phi(x_i)](\cdot)=\sum_{i=1}^n\alpha_i\,k(\cdot,x_i).

Step 3 — the penalty term punishes vv. Because u⊥vu\perp v, the Pythagorean theorem gives

∥f∥=∥u+v∥=∥u∥2+2⟨u,v⟩⏟=0+∥v∥2=∥u∥2+∥v∥2≥∥u∥2=∥u∥, \|f\| = \|u+v\| = \sqrt{\|u\|^2 + 2\underbrace{\langle u,v\rangle}_{=0} + \|v\|^2} = \sqrt{\|u\|^2+\|v\|^2}\ \ge\ \sqrt{\|u\|^2}=\|u\|,

with equality iff v=0v=0. Since gg is strictly increasing,

g(∥f∥)=g(∥u∥2+∥v∥2)≥g(∥u∥2)=g(∥u∥). g(\|f\|) = g\Big(\sqrt{\|u\|^2+\|v\|^2}\Big)\ \ge\ g\Big(\sqrt{\|u\|^2}\Big)=g(\|u\|).

So any nonzero vv strictly increases the regularizer while leaving the fit untouched — pure loss.

Conclusion. The first term is independent of vv; the second is minimized by v=0v=0. Therefore the minimizer must have v=0v=0, giving

f*(⋅)=u(⋅)=∑i=1nαik(⋅,xi).◼ f^{*}(\cdot)=u(\cdot)=\sum_{i=1}^{n}\alpha_i\,k(\cdot,x_i).\qquad\blacksquare

The one-sentence version. Any part of a candidate function that sticks out of the span of the training features is invisible to the data but visible to the penalty — so the optimum throws it away, and what remains is a finite kernel expansion.

Corollary (matrix form). Stacking the training predictions into a vector, with the kernel matrix K(X,X)K(X,X) where [K(X,X)]ij=k(xi,xj)[K(X,X)]_{ij}=k(x_i,x_j),

ŷ=K(X,X)α. \hat{y} = K(X,X)\,\alpha.

3.4 Application: Kernel Ridge Regression (full derivation)

Let's turn the theorem into a concrete, closed-form algorithm — this is the derivation the slides walk through, with every step shown.

Setup. Data 𝒟={(xi,yi)}i=1n\mathcal{D}=\{(x_i,y_i)\}_{i=1}^n with xi∈ℝd,yi∈ℝx_i\in\mathbb{R}^d,\ y_i\in\mathbb{R}. Use the squared-error loss ℒ(f(xi),yi)=(f(xi)−yi)2\mathcal{L}(f(x_i),y_i)=(f(x_i)-y_i)^2 and the squared-norm regularizer g(∥f∥)=λ∥f∥2g(\|f\|)=\lambda\|f\|^2 for some λ>0\lambda>0:

f*=argminf∈ℱ1n∑i=1n(f(xi)−yi)2+λ∥f∥2. f^{*}=\arg\min_{f\in\mathcal{F}}\ \frac1n\sum_{i=1}^n (f(x_i)-y_i)^2 + \lambda\|f\|^2 .

Step 1 — invoke the representer theorem. We know f*(⋅)=∑i=1nαik(⋅,xi)f^{*}(\cdot)=\sum_{i=1}^n\alpha_i k(\cdot,x_i) for some α=(α1,…,αn)⊤∈ℝn\alpha=(\alpha_1,\dots,\alpha_n)^{\top}\in\mathbb{R}^n. Now we just need the best α\alpha.

Step 2 — rewrite everything in terms of α\alpha and KK. Let K=K(X,X)K=K(X,X) (an n×nn\times n symmetric matrix). Two facts:

  • Predictions: f*(xj)=∑iαik(xj,xi)=(Kα)jf^{*}(x_j)=\sum_i\alpha_i k(x_j,x_i)=(K\alpha)_j, so the vector of fitted values is KαK\alpha.
  • Norm: using f*=∑iαiϕ(xi)f^{*}=\sum_i\alpha_i\phi(x_i), ∥f*∥2=⟨∑iαiϕ(xi),∑jαjϕ(xj)⟩=∑i,jαiαjk(xi,xj)=α⊤Kα. \|f^{*}\|^2 = \Big\langle \sum_i\alpha_i\phi(x_i),\ \sum_j\alpha_j\phi(x_j)\Big\rangle = \sum_{i,j}\alpha_i\alpha_j\,k(x_i,x_j) = \alpha^{\top}K\alpha .

Substitute into the objective and expand the squared norm (using K⊤=KK^{\top}=K):

h(α):=1n∥Kα−y∥2+λα⊤Kα=1n[α⊤K2α−2α⊤Ky+y⊤y⏟∥Kα−y∥2]+λα⊤Kα. h(\alpha):=\frac1n\|K\alpha - y\|^2 + \lambda\,\alpha^{\top}K\alpha = \frac1n\Big[\underbrace{\alpha^{\top}K^2\alpha - 2\alpha^{\top}Ky + y^{\top}y}_{\|K\alpha-y\|^2}\Big] + \lambda\,\alpha^{\top}K\alpha .

Step 3 — take the gradient and set it to zero. Using ∇α(α⊤Mα)=2Mα\nabla_\alpha(\alpha^{\top}M\alpha)=2M\alpha for symmetric MM, and ∇α(α⊤b)=b\nabla_\alpha(\alpha^{\top}b)=b:

∇αh(α)=1n[2K2α−2Ky]+2λKα=!0. \nabla_\alpha h(\alpha) = \frac1n\big[2K^2\alpha - 2Ky\big] + 2\lambda K\alpha \overset{!}{=}0.

Multiply by n2\tfrac{n}{2} and rearrange:

K2α−Ky+nλKα=0⇔K2α+nλKα=Ky. K^2\alpha - Ky + n\lambda K\alpha = 0 \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad K^2\alpha + n\lambda K\alpha = Ky .

Factor a KK on the left, K(Kα+nλα)=KyK\big(K\alpha + n\lambda\alpha\big)=Ky. Assuming KK is invertible, cancel it:

(K+nλI)α=y⇒α*=(K+nλI)−1y. (K + n\lambda I)\,\alpha = y \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \boxed{\,\alpha^{*} = (K + n\lambda I)^{-1}\,y\,}.

A free bonus of regularization. Even if KK is singular (e.g. duplicate points), K+nλIK+n\lambda I is always invertible for λ>0\lambda>0, because adding nλIn\lambda I shifts every eigenvalue up by nλ>0n\lambda>0. Regularization doesn't just fight overfitting — it makes the linear system solvable.

Step 4 — predict at a new point xx. Plug α*\alpha^{*} back into the kernel expansion. With the row vector K(x,X)=(k(x,x1),…,k(x,xn))∈ℝnK(x,X)=\big(k(x,x_1),\dots,k(x,x_n)\big)\in\mathbb{R}^n,

f*(x)=K(x,X)α*=K(x,X)(K(X,X)+nλI)−1y. \boxed{\,f^{*}(x)=K(x,X)\,\alpha^{*}=K(x,X)\,(K(X,X)+n\lambda I)^{-1}\,y\,}.

Kernel ridge regression on noisy data with an RBF kernel. The faint grey curves are the individual weighted bumps αik(⋅,xi)\alpha_i k(\cdot,x_i); the bold curve is their sum f*f^{*}. Exactly the shape the representer theorem promised — one bump per data point, combined with the learned weights α*\alpha^{*}.

Notice what never appeared: the feature vectors ϕ(xi)\phi(x_i), the dimension pp of feature space, or ww. Every quantity is expressed through kernel evaluations only. That is the kernel trick paying off — we optimized in a possibly infinite-dimensional space using an n×nn\times n system.

3.5 Cost: input space vs. feature space

Why kernelize? Compare the ways to solve (regularized) linear regression. Let X∈ℝn×dX\in\mathbb{R}^{n\times d} be the data in input space and Z∈ℝn×pZ\in\mathbb{R}^{n\times p} the data in an explicit feature space of dimension pp.

Method Solution Cost
Linear regression in ℝd\mathbb{R}^d w*=(X⊤X)−1X⊤yw^{*}=(X^{\top}X)^{-1}X^{\top}y 𝒪(d2n+d3)\mathcal{O}(d^2 n + d^3)
Linear regression in feature space ℱ\mathcal{F} w*=(Z⊤Z)−1Z⊤yw^{*}=(Z^{\top}Z)^{-1}Z^{\top}y 𝒪(p2n+p3)\mathcal{O}(p^2 n + p^3)
Kernel regression α*=K−1y\alpha^{*}=K^{-1}y, then ŷ=Kα\hat y = K\alpha 𝒪(n2d+n3)\mathcal{O}(n^2 d + n^3)

The trade. Explicit features cost 𝒪(p3)\mathcal{O}(p^3) — hopeless when pp is huge or infinite. The kernel form costs 𝒪(n3)\mathcal{O}(n^3), independent of pp. So kernels win big when the feature space is enormous but the dataset is moderate (n≪pn \ll p). The flip side: when nn is very large, the n×nn\times n kernel matrix becomes the bottleneck — that's when you'd go back to explicit features or approximations. In the kernel view we compute α*\alpha^{*} directly and the explicit weight vector w*w^{*} is simply avoided.

Where does the finite expansion come from without an RKHS? Even plain gradient descent gives a hint. If we ever represent the weights as a linear combination of the training features,

w=∑i=1nαiϕ(xi)=Z⊤α,Z=(ϕ(x1),…,ϕ(xn))⊤∈ℝn×p, w=\sum_{i=1}^n\alpha_i\phi(x_i)=Z^{\top}\alpha,\qquad Z=(\phi(x_1),\dots,\phi(x_n))^{\top}\in\mathbb{R}^{n\times p},

then any update of the form g(Zw)g(Zw) becomes g(ZZ⊤α)=g(K(X,X)α)g(ZZ^{\top}\alpha)=g\big(K(X,X)\,\alpha\big) — again only kernels appear. The representer theorem is the general principle that guarantees this always happens for regularized empirical risk. (For example, an L2L_2-regularized linear model ℒ(w)=∑iℓi(w⊤xi,yi)+λ2∥w∥2\mathcal{L}(w)=\sum_i \ell_i(w^{\top}x_i,y_i)+\tfrac{\lambda}{2}\|w\|^2 has optimum w*=∑iαixi=X⊤αw^{*}=\sum_i \alpha_i x_i = X^{\top}\alpha with αi=−λ−1ℓi′((w*)⊤xi,yi)\alpha_i=-\lambda^{-1}\ell_i'((w^{*})^{\top}x_i,y_i) — a linear combination of the training points, exactly as promised.)

Part 4 — Regularization

The representer theorem required a regularizer g(∥f∥)g(\|f\|). Part 4 is about what that term actually does, and the two most important choices for it: L2L_2 and L0L_0.

4.1 Why regularize?

Complex models fit training data beautifully and then fail on new data — overfitting.

Fitting polynomials of increasing degree to the same 11 noisy points from a sine curve (green dashed = truth). Degree 1 is too rigid (underfits). Degree 3 captures the shape (good). Degree 9 threads every point but oscillates wildly between them (overfits) — it memorized the noise.

But we often genuinely need complex models: the true relationship between xix_i and yiy_i may require a high-degree polynomial or many interacting features. So we can't just "keep it simple." Our two main tools:

  • Regularization — add a penalty on model complexity (this Part).
  • Ensembling — average many models to cut variance (a later topic).

Which model should you pick? Suppose two linear fits have the same training error:

A steep line (red, large ∥w∥\|w\|) and a shallow line (green, small ∥w∥\|w\|) with essentially identical training error. Prefer the green one.

Why prefer the flatter line? Because a smaller slope means a small change in xix_i produces only a small change in the prediction yiy_i; the model is less sensitive to getting ww exactly right, and its test error is typically lower. Large weights make a model twitchy and fragile. Concretely, the same degree-7 curve can be written with tame or wild coefficients:

ŷi=0.0001xi7+0.03xi3+3vs.ŷi=1000xi7−500xi6+890xi. \hat{y}_i = 0.0001\,x_i^{7} + 0.03\,x_i^{3} + 3 \qquad\text{vs.}\qquad \hat{y}_i = 1000\,x_i^{7} - 500\,x_i^{6} + 890\,x_i .

Both are degree-7 polynomials; the left one is smooth and stable, the right one is a rollercoaster. Regularization is the mechanism that steers us toward the tame version by penalizing large weights.

4.2 L2L_2 regularization (ridge)

The standard choice. Add the squared L2L_2-norm of the weights to the squared-error loss:

ℒ(w)=12∑i=1n(w⊤xi−yi)2+λ2∑j=1dwj2⇔ℒ(w)=12∥Xw−y∥2+λ2∥w∥2. \mathcal{L}(w)=\frac12\sum_{i=1}^n (w^{\top}x_i - y_i)^2 + \frac{\lambda}{2}\sum_{j=1}^{d} w_j^2 \qquad\Longleftrightarrow\qquad \mathcal{L}(w)=\frac12\|Xw-y\|^2 + \frac{\lambda}{2}\|w\|^2 .

Here λ>0\lambda>0 is the regularization parameter.

The mantra. Regularized loss balances low error against small weights: "you may increase the training error, if doing so makes ww smaller." This nearly always reduces overfitting.

The fundamental trade-off, stated crisply:

  • Regularization increases training error (you're no longer free to chase every point).
  • Regularization decreases approximation (generalization) error — up to a point.

As λ\lambda grows (left to right), training error rises monotonically. Test error is U-shaped: too little regularization overfits (high variance), too much underfits (high bias). The sweet spot in the middle is the λ\lambda we're hunting for.

The same story, shown as actual fitted curves (here with a kernel model, but the intuition is identical for linear ridge):

Left: a tiny λ\lambda lets the model chase every point — wiggly, overfit. Right: a huge λ\lambda forces the weights so small the curve goes nearly flat — underfit. Middle: a balanced λ\lambda recovers the smooth underlying trend.

The geometry. In weight space, the squared-error term has elliptical contours around the unregularized optimum. The penalty λ2∥w∥2\tfrac{\lambda}{2}\|w\|^2 pulls the solution toward the origin:

Blue ellipses are contours of equal squared error (center = unregularized w*w^{*}, red). Adding the L2L_2 penalty moves the solution to the green point — same ballpark training error, but noticeably smaller weights. The arrow is the shrinkage L2L_2 applies.

Solving L2L_2 regression exactly

Unregularized first. Expand the objective ℒ(w)=12∥Xw−y∥2\mathcal{L}(w)=\tfrac12\|Xw-y\|^2:

ℒ(w)=12w⊤X⊤Xw−w⊤X⊤y+12y⊤y. \mathcal{L}(w)=\frac12 w^{\top}X^{\top}Xw - w^{\top}X^{\top}y + \frac12 y^{\top}y .

Gradient, then set to zero (these are the normal equations):

∇wℒ(w)=X⊤Xw−X⊤y=!0⇔X⊤Xw=X⊤y⇒w=(X⊤X)−1X⊤y(if X⊤X invertible). \nabla_w\mathcal{L}(w)=X^{\top}Xw - X^{\top}y \overset{!}{=}0 \ \Longleftrightarrow\ X^{\top}Xw=X^{\top}y \ \Longrightarrow\ w=(X^{\top}X)^{-1}X^{\top}y \quad(\text{if }X^{\top}X\text{ invertible}).

Now regularized. Add λ2∥w∥2=λ2w⊤w\tfrac{\lambda}{2}\|w\|^2=\tfrac{\lambda}{2}w^{\top}w. The objective and gradient pick up one term each (shown in red conceptually):

ℒ(w)=12∥Xw−y∥2+λ2w⊤w,∇wℒ(w)=X⊤Xw−X⊤y+λw. \mathcal{L}(w)=\frac12\|Xw-y\|^2 + \frac{\lambda}{2}w^{\top}w, \qquad \nabla_w\mathcal{L}(w)=X^{\top}Xw - X^{\top}y + \lambda w .

Set to zero:

X⊤Xw−X⊤y+λw=0⇔(X⊤X+λI)w=X⊤y⇒w=(X⊤X+λI)−1X⊤y. X^{\top}Xw - X^{\top}y + \lambda w = 0 \ \Longleftrightarrow\ (X^{\top}X + \lambda I)\,w = X^{\top}y \ \Longrightarrow\ \boxed{\,w=(X^{\top}X + \lambda I)^{-1}X^{\top}y\,}.

As noted before, X⊤X+λIX^{\top}X + \lambda I is always invertible for λ>0\lambda>0. (Compare with kernel ridge in §3.4: same idea, dual variables.)

By gradient descent (when dd is large and you'd rather not invert a matrix): with the gradient above, the update rule is

w(t+1)←w(t)−γ∇wℒ(w)=w(t)−γ[X⊤(Xw(t)−y)+λw(t)], w^{(t+1)} \leftarrow w^{(t)} - \gamma\,\nabla_w\mathcal{L}(w) = w^{(t)} - \gamma\big[X^{\top}(Xw^{(t)}-y) + \lambda w^{(t)}\big],

at a cost of only 𝒪(nd)\mathcal{O}(nd) per iteration (the unregularized version drops the λw(t)\lambda w^{(t)} term).

What λ\lambda actually does — numbers

The regularization parameter λ\lambda is a hyperparameter controlling the strength: large λ\lambda puts a large penalty on the weights, so training error goes up and approximation error goes down. Here is a real ridge solution on a small 5-feature dataset as λ\lambda sweeps (computed, not sketched):

λ\lambda w1w_1 w2w_2 w3w_3 w4w_4 w5w_5 |Xw−y|2|Xw-y|^2 |w|2|w|^2
0 1.98 −1.40 1.06 0.45 −0.78 8.77 7.81
1 1.89 −1.31 0.99 0.48 −0.74 9.14 7.06
4 1.67 −1.12 0.83 0.53 −0.64 13.04 5.42
16 1.17 −0.73 0.48 0.53 −0.43 37.92 2.59
64 0.56 −0.32 0.16 0.34 −0.18 100.40 0.59
256 0.19 −0.10 0.04 0.12 −0.05 158.69 0.07
1024 0.05 −0.03 0.01 0.04 −0.01 184.12 0.00

Read the two rightmost columns: as λ\lambda grows, the fit error ∥Xw−y∥2\|Xw-y\|^2 increases and the weight size ∥w∥2\|w\|^2 decreases — the trade-off made numeric. At λ=0\lambda=0 we recover ordinary least squares; as λ→∞\lambda\to\infty every weight is crushed toward 00. Note individual wjw_j can move either way, but because we penalize the squared norm, the largest weights are shrunk the most (squaring makes big weights expensive).

Plotting the weights against λ\lambda gives the regularization path:

Each curve is one coefficient wjw_j as λ\lambda increases. The path starts at the unregularized least-squares solution (λ=0\lambda=0, left) and every coefficient is smoothly pulled toward 00 as λ→∞\lambda\to\infty.

How to choose λ\lambda?

  • Theory: as the sample size nn grows, a good λ\lambda lies roughly in the range 𝒪(1)\mathcal{O}(1) to 𝒪(n)\mathcal{O}(\sqrt{n}).
  • Practice: pick the λ\lambda that minimizes validation error (or cross-validation error). This is the U-curve from the trade-off figure, evaluated on held-out data.

4.3 L0L_0 regularization: feature selection

L2L_2 shrinks weights but rarely sets them exactly to zero. Sometimes what we really want is to throw entire features away — this is feature selection, and it corresponds to a different penalty.

The dilemma. When we build a model we must decide which features to use:

  • Too few features → not enough information → bad predictions. (Predicting spam from only whether the word "buy" appears.)
  • Too many features → the model latches onto irrelevant noise → bad predictions. (Predicting spam from the temperature on the day the email was sent. Add enough random features and your training error hits zero — while learning nothing.)

Among many candidate features x⋅jx_{\cdot j}, we want to select the ones that actually help predict yy. "Number of features" is itself a hyperparameter: more features → lower training error but higher approximation error.

Search and score. The standard framework:

  1. Define a score function score(S)\text{score}(S) measuring the quality of a feature subset SS (e.g. a loss).
  2. Search for the subset SS with the best score.

For a linear regression model, a natural score is the squared error using only the features in SS:

score(S)=12∑i=1n(wS⊤xi∣S−yi)2, \text{score}(S)=\frac12\sum_{i=1}^n\big(w_S^{\top}x_{i\mid S} - y_i\big)^2,

where xi∣Sx_{i\mid S} keeps only the features in SS and wSw_S are the corresponding weights.

On which data do we score? Not the training set — training error only ever goes down as you add features, so scoring on training data always selects all features. Scoring on a validation set is better (find the subset with lowest validation error, which is what minimizes test error). But there is a subtlety: with dd features there are 2d2^d subsets, and searching all of them invites optimization bias / false positives — some irrelevant feature will help by chance on the validation set.

The fix: a complexity penalty. Charge for every feature you include:

score(S)=12∑i=1n(wS⊤xi∣S−yi)2+λ⋅size(S). \text{score}(S)=\frac12\sum_{i=1}^n\big(w_S^{\top}x_{i\mid S}-y_i\big)^2 + \lambda\cdot\text{size}(S).

Now, if two subsets have similar squared error, the score prefers the smaller one. In words: "you may add a feature, but only if it reduces the training error by at least λ\lambda." (Setting w1=10−5w_1=10^{-5} has a negligible effect on the error, so it's better to drop that feature entirely.)

Connecting to a norm. In a linear model, setting wj=0w_j=0 is exactly the same as removing feature x⋅jx_{\cdot j}:

ŷi=w1xi1+w2xi2+w3xi3+…→w2=0ŷi=w1xi1+0⋅xi2+w3xi3+…=w1xi1+w3xi3+… \hat y_i = w_1 x_{i1}+w_2 x_{i2}+w_3 x_{i3}+\dots \ \xrightarrow{\ w_2=0\ }\ \hat y_i = w_1 x_{i1}+0\cdot x_{i2}+w_3 x_{i3}+\dots = w_1 x_{i1}+w_3 x_{i3}+\dots

The L0L_0 "norm" counts the nonzero entries of a vector, so it equals the number of features used:

∥w∥0=#{j:wj≠0}=size(S).e.g. (1,0,2,0,3)⊤⇒∥w∥0=3,(0,0,0,0,0)⊤⇒∥w∥0=0. \|w\|_0 = \#\{j : w_j\neq 0\} = \text{size}(S). \qquad \text{e.g. } (1,0,2,0,3)^{\top}\Rightarrow\|w\|_0=3,\quad (0,0,0,0,0)^{\top}\Rightarrow\|w\|_0=0.

(It's not a true norm — it isn't homogeneous — hence the quotes.) A small ∥w∥0\|w\|_0 means the model uses few features. Putting it together, feature selection with a complexity penalty is L0L_0-regularized regression:

ℒ(w)=12∥Xw−y∥2⏟prediction error+λ∥w∥0⏟degrees of freedom \boxed{\ \mathcal{L}(w)=\underbrace{\frac12\|Xw-y\|^2}_{\text{prediction error}} + \underbrace{\lambda\|w\|_0}_{\text{degrees of freedom}}\ }

Its behavior across λ\lambda:

  • λ=0\lambda=0: no penalty on nonzeros → minimize plain squared loss → use all features.
  • λ=∞\lambda=\infty: infinite penalty → minimum at w=0w=0 → use no features.
  • 0<λ<∞0<\lambda<\infty: larger λ\lambda emphasizes zeros → more features removed.

Training error (blue) drops monotonically as you add features. Validation error (orange) is U-shaped — too few features underfit, too many overfit. L0L_0 regularization, with λ\lambda tuned, targets the bottom of that U.

Optimization is hard. Finding the best subset means searching 2d2^d possibilities — infeasible. A practical greedy method is forward selection:

  1. Start with empty sets S={}S=\{\} and best-so-far B={}B=\{\}.
  2. For each feature x⋅j′x_{\cdot j'} not yet in SS: compute the score of S∪x⋅j′S\cup x_{\cdot j'}.
  3. Find the feature x⋅jx_{\cdot j} with the best score in step 2.
  4. If S∪x⋅jS\cup x_{\cdot j} improves on the best score seen so far, set B←S∪x⋅jB\leftarrow S\cup x_{\cdot j}.
  5. If some feature is still not in SS: set S←S∪x⋅jS\leftarrow S\cup x_{\cdot j} and go back to step 2.
  6. Return the best feature set BB.

Runtime. Each of the (up to) dd steps fits up to dd models, so forward selection fits 𝒪(d2)\mathcal{O}(d^2) models instead of 2d2^d. Total cost is 𝒪(d2)\mathcal{O}(d^2) times the cost of one fit — for L0L_0-regularized squared loss that's 𝒪(nd4+d5)\mathcal{O}(nd^4+d^5), since fitting a single regression model costs 𝒪(nd2+d3)\mathcal{O}(nd^2+d^3). Forward selection is not guaranteed to find the globally best subset, but fitting far fewer models makes it cheaper, less prone to overfitting, and less prone to false positives.

4.4 Beyond the slides: a word on L1L_1 / Lasso

(Not in the decks, but the question always comes up, so here's the bridge.) L0L_0 gives true sparsity but is combinatorially hard. L2L_2 is easy but doesn't zero out features. L1L_1 regularization (Lasso) is the best-of-both compromise:

ℒ(w)=12∥Xw−y∥2+λ∥w∥1,∥w∥1=∑j|wj|. \mathcal{L}(w)=\frac12\|Xw-y\|^2 + \lambda\|w\|_1,\qquad \|w\|_1=\sum_j |w_j|.

It is convex (so tractable, unlike L0L_0) yet its diamond-shaped constraint has sharp corners on the axes, so the optimum often lands exactly on a corner — driving some wjw_j to exactly zero and thus selecting features automatically. Think of it as a convex relaxation of L0L_0 that keeps sparsity. It slots neatly between the L0L_0 and L2L_2 stories above.

Part 5 — Putting it all together

You now have the full arc, from an abstract similarity function to a regularized, computable learning algorithm.

Kernel theory.

  • An RKHS is a Hilbert space of functions in which pointwise evaluation is continuous — equivalently, a space where inner products correspond to kernel evaluations, k(x,x′)=⟨ϕ(x),ϕ(x′)⟩k(x,x')=\langle\phi(x),\phi(x')\rangle.
  • The reproducing kernel falls out of the Riesz representation theorem: f(x)=⟨f,kx⟩f(x)=\langle f,k_x\rangle, and k(x,x′)=⟨kx,kx′⟩k(x,x')=\langle k_x,k_{x'}\rangle is automatically symmetric and positive-definite.
  • Moore–Aronszajn: every symmetric positive-definite kernel is the reproducing kernel of a unique RKHS. Pick a kernel and you've picked a feature space — the kernel trick.

Representer theorem.

  • It describes the functional form of the minimizer of the regularized empirical risk: a finite linear combination of kernel evaluations, f*(⋅)=∑i=1nαik(⋅,xi)f^{*}(\cdot)=\sum_{i=1}^n\alpha_i k(\cdot,x_i).
  • It is the theoretical foundation for kernelizing linear methods, and it turns an infinite-dimensional search into solving for nn numbers.
  • Application: kernel ridge regression, α*=(K+nλI)−1y\alpha^{*}=(K+n\lambda I)^{-1}y, with predictions f*(x)=K(x,X)α*f^{*}(x)=K(x,X)\,\alpha^{*} — computed purely through kernels.

Regularization.

  • L2L_2 (ridge): shrinks weights toward zero for robustness and stability; exact solution via the modified normal equations w=(X⊤X+λI)−1X⊤yw=(X^{\top}X+\lambda I)^{-1}X^{\top}y; λ\lambda chosen by (cross-)validation.
  • L0L_0: counts nonzero weights, i.e. selects relevant features; optimized greedily by forward selection.
  • Both are instances of the same principle from the representer theorem's g(∥f∥)g(\|f\|) term: pay for complexity, and generalization improves.

The single thread through both lectures. Choosing a kernel picks a feature space; regularization tells the representer theorem which function in that space to trust; the result is a finite, kernel-only formula you can actually compute.

Built from the two THWS "Machine Learning" decks (Kernel Theory; Regularization). All figures were generated programmatically with Python/matplotlib for accuracy — no numbers or curves were sketched by hand.
